behavior_summary: This module is a purely combinational even-parity encoder with interface-only clock and reset ports. For any cycle in which data_valid is 1, it computes parity as the XOR reduction of data[11:0], then drives enc_codeword = {parity, data[11:0]}. Because parity equals ^data, the full 13-bit codeword always contains an even number of 1 bits. enc_valid directly mirrors data_valid with no cycle latency. When data_valid is 0, enc_valid must be 0 and enc_codeword is unspecified and should not be checked.
edge_cases: clk and rst are present in the interface but do not affect encoding behavior; toggling either must not change enc_valid or enc_codeword except through other inputs.
When data_valid is 1 and data has an odd number of 1s, parity must be 1 so the 13-bit codeword has even parity.
When data_valid is 1 and data has an even number of 1s, parity must be 0 so the 13-bit codeword remains even parity.
enc_codeword[12] is the parity bit and enc_codeword[11:0] must exactly equal data[11:0]; no bit reordering, inversion, or shifting is allowed.
When data_valid is 0, enc_valid must be 0 and enc_codeword is not guaranteed; a testbench should avoid strict checking of enc_codeword in that case.
There is no sequential state, no buffering, and no one-cycle delay; outputs are same-cycle combinational functions of inputs.
Overflow, underflow, saturation, and wrap-around do not apply because the logic only computes parity and concatenation.
priorities: data_valid gates only output validity, not the parity calculation rule itself
No control-signal priority tree exists because the design is combinational and has no mutually competing commands
Functional interpretation priority for checking is: if data_valid == 1, check enc_valid == 1 and enc_codeword == {^data, data}; if data_valid == 0, check only enc_valid == 0
mutation_risks: Parity computed with the wrong polarity, e.g. using ~(^data) instead of ^data
Parity bit appended at LSB instead of prepended at MSB
Data bits copied into enc_codeword in the wrong order or wrong slice
enc_valid implemented as a registered signal or delayed by one cycle instead of directly following data_valid
clk or rst incorrectly inserted into the functional logic, causing outputs to change with clock edges or reset assertion
Parity computed over 13 bits, 11 bits, or some incorrect subset of data bits instead of exactly all 12 data bits
enc_codeword forced to zero or held stable when data_valid is 0 despite being specified as don't-care
Use of XNOR/reduction inversion causing odd-parity rather than even-parity codeword
